title: pH and pOH for Strong Acids and Bases
subtitle: Turning concentration into pH, and back again
subject: Chemistry
syllabusDotPoints: conduct a practical investigation to measure the pH of a range of acids and bases
calculate pH, pOH, hydrogen ion concentration ([Hâپ؛]) and hydroxide ion concentration ([OHâپ»]) for a range of solutions (ACSCH102)
inquiryQuestion: What is the role of water in solutions of acids and bases?
lessonIntent: Students can calculate pH and pOH for strong acids and bases, including diprotic species, dilution and mixing.
examSkill: Find [Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛] or [OHâپ»] from concentration (أ—2 for diprotic), convert with pH = âˆ’log and pH + pOH = 14, and use câ‚پVâ‚پ = câ‚‚Vâ‚‚ and V(total) for dilution and mixing.

introVoiceover.text: Welcome to HSC Science. This is HSC Chemistry. Today, calculating pH and pOH for strong acids and bases. Let's get into it.

scenes.caption: pH and pOH for Strong Acids and Bases (Year 12 Chemistry, Module 6, Lesson 8)

scenes.heading: A tiny pH shift, a big deal
scenes.body: At altitude, a climber's blood pH rises just 0.17. Yet that's a 32% drop in hydrogen ions, enough to be dangerous.
scenes.callout: How can a small pH change mean a huge change in [Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛]?
scenes.caption: A 0.17 pH rise is a 32% fall in [Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛] because the scale is logarithmic.

voiceover.text: OK, here's why this maths actually matters. High on a mountain, a climber hyperventilates, driving carbon dioxide out of the blood, and their blood pH climbs from about seven-point-four-five to seven-point-six-two. That looks like a tiny shift, barely zero-point-two of a pH unit. But because the pH scale is logarithmic, it's actually a thirty-two percent drop in hydrogen ion concentration, enough to cause muscle spasms, confusion, even cardiac problems. Today you'll learn the maths behind that. How to turn a concentration into a pH, how to go backwards from pH to concentration, and how to handle dilution and mixing of strong acids and bases. The logarithm is the whole story.

scenes.heading: The pH scale is logarithmic
scenes.body: pH = âˆ’log[Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛], so every single pH unit means a tenfold change in hydrogen ions.

bullets.text: pH 3 has ten times more Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛ than pH 4, and a hundred times more than pH 5.
Lower pH means more acidic, which means more Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛.
To go back, [Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛] = 10 to the power of minus pH.

scenes.secondary: So pH 3 isn't 'twice' pH 6, it's a thousand times more acidic. Always convert a pH gap into a power of ten.
scenes.callout: A difference of n pH units is a 10âپ؟-fold difference in [Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛].
scenes.caption: pH = âˆ’log[Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛]; each unit is 10أ— in [Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛]; lower pH = more acidic.

voiceover.text: Let's get the scale straight first. pH is defined as the negative log, base ten, of the hydrogen ion concentration. The negative sign just turns those tiny decimal concentrations into friendly positive numbers. The key consequence of the log is that each single pH unit is a tenfold change in hydrogen ion concentration. So a solution at pH three has ten times more hydrogen ions than pH four, and a hundred times more than pH five, even though they look close on the scale. And the direction trips people up, so burn this in, lower pH means more acidic, which means more hydrogen ions. To go backwards from a pH to a concentration, raise ten to the power of minus pH. The big habit to build, never say pH three is twice as acidic as pH six. It's a thousand times more acidic. Always convert a pH gap into a power of ten.

scenes.heading: The formulas you need

bullets.text: pOH = âˆ’log[OHâپ»]; at 25 آ°C, pH + pOH = 14.
Strong acid: [Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛] = c, but أ—2 for dilute Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„.
Strong base: [OHâپ»] = c, but أ—2 for Ca(OH)â‚‚ and Ba(OH)â‚‚.

scenes.callout: Count the ionisable protons or hydroxides before you take a log.
scenes.caption: pH = âˆ’log[Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛]; pOH = âˆ’log[OHâپ»]; pH+pOH=14; أ—2 for diprotic.

voiceover.text: Here are the formulas. pH is minus the log of the hydronium concentration, and to reverse it, the hydronium concentration is ten to the power of minus pH. The same pattern gives pOH from the hydroxide concentration, and at twenty-five degrees, pH plus pOH equals fourteen, which is the bridge between them. For a strong acid, hydronium concentration equals the acid concentration, except for dilute sulfuric acid, where it's twice the concentration because each molecule gives two protons. For a strong base, hydroxide concentration equals the base concentration, except for calcium and barium hydroxide, where it's twice, because each formula unit gives two hydroxides. The single habit that protects your marks, count the ionisable protons or hydroxides before you ever take a log.

scenes.heading: Acid is direct; base goes via pOH
scenes.body: Strong acids give pH straight away; strong bases need one extra step through pOH.

bullets.text: Acid: [Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛] = c (أ—2 for Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„), then pH = âˆ’log[Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛].
Base: [OHâپ»] = c (أ—2 for group 2), then pOH = âˆ’log[OHâپ»].
Finish the base with pH = 14 âˆ’ pOH at 25 آ°C.

scenes.secondary: Sanity-check every answer: an acid must land below pH 7, a base above it. If a base gives pH below 7, you forgot 14 minus pOH.
scenes.callout: Never use Ka for a strong acid; it ionises completely, so [Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛] = c.
scenes.caption: Acid: [Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛]â†’pH directly. Base: [OHâپ»]â†’pOHâ†’pH = 14 âˆ’ pOH.

voiceover.text: Now the two routes. For a strong acid it's direct. The hydronium concentration just is the acid concentration, doubling for sulfuric acid, and then pH is minus the log of that. For a strong base there's one extra step, because you start with hydroxide, not hydronium. You find the hydroxide concentration, doubling for calcium or barium hydroxide, then take pOH as minus its log, and finally convert with pH equals fourteen minus pOH. Always sanity-check. An acid must come out below pH seven, a base above seven. If your base calculation gives something below seven, you almost certainly forgot the fourteen-minus-pOH step. And one category error to avoid, never use Ka for a strong acid. Ka is for weak acids only. A strong acid is fully ionised, so its hydronium concentration simply equals its concentration.

scenes.heading: Acid and base pH
scenes.question: Find the pH at 25 آ°C of: (a) 0.025 mol Lâپ»آ¹ HNOâ‚ƒ, (b) 0.0040 mol Lâپ»آ¹ Ca(OH)â‚‚.
scenes.coachNote: HNOâ‚ƒ is monoprotic; Ca(OH)â‚‚ gives two OHâپ».
steps: (b) Ca(OH)â‚‚ strong, two OHâپ»: [OHâپ»] = 2 أ— 0.0040 = 0.0080 mol Lâپ»آ¹
scenes.caption: 0.025 M HNOâ‚ƒ â†’ pH 1.60; 0.0040 M Ca(OH)â‚‚ â†’ pH 11.90.

voiceover.text: Let's do one of each. Part a, nought-point-zero-two-five molar nitric acid. It's a strong, monoprotic acid, so the hydronium concentration is just nought-point-zero-two-five. The pH is minus the log of that, which is one-point-six-zero, comfortably below seven, good. Part b, nought-point-zero-zero-four molar calcium hydroxide. It's a strong base, but each formula unit gives two hydroxides, so the hydroxide concentration is two times nought-point-zero-zero-four, which is nought-point-zero-zero-eight. The pOH is minus the log of that, which is two-point-one-zero. Then convert, pH equals fourteen minus two-point-one-zero, giving eleven-point-nine-zero, nicely above seven. So nitric acid gives pH one-point-six, and calcium hydroxide gives pH eleven-point-nine. The doubling and the fourteen-minus step are where the marks live.

scenes.heading: Mixing acid and base
scenes.question: Mix 40.0 mL of 0.150 mol Lâپ»آ¹ HCl with 60.0 mL of 0.0800 mol Lâپ»آ¹ Ba(OH)â‚‚. Find the pH.
scenes.coachNote: Count moles, find the excess, divide by total volume.
steps: n(OHâپ») = 0.0800 أ— 0.0600 أ— 2 = 9.60 أ— 10âپ»آ³ mol (Ba(OH)â‚‚ gives 2 OHâپ»)
Excess OHâپ» = 9.60 أ— 10âپ»آ³ âˆ’ 6.00 أ— 10âپ»آ³ = 3.60 أ— 10âپ»آ³ mol
scenes.caption: Excess OHâپ» 3.60أ—10âپ»آ³ mol in 100 mL â†’ pH 12.56.

voiceover.text: Now a mixing problem, where you find who wins. Forty millilitres of nought-point-one-five molar hydrochloric acid meets sixty millilitres of nought-point-zero-eight molar barium hydroxide. Step one, moles of hydrogen ion, nought-point-one-five times nought-point-zero-four, which is six times ten to the minus three. Step two, moles of hydroxide, and watch the trap, barium hydroxide gives two hydroxides, so it's nought-point-zero-eight times nought-point-zero-six times two, which is nine-point-six times ten to the minus three. Step three, hydroxide wins, and the excess is nine-point-six minus six, that's three-point-six times ten to the minus three moles. Step four, the total volume is a hundred millilitres, so the excess hydroxide concentration is three-point-six times ten to the minus three over nought-point-one, which is nought-point-zero-three-six. Step five, pOH is minus the log, one-point-four-four, so pH is fourteen minus that, twelve-point-five-six. Two traps caught, the doubling and the combined volume.

scenes.heading: Three traps
scenes.body: Trap 1: giving 0.10 mol Lâپ»آ¹ Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„ a pH of 1.0. It's diprotic, so [Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛] = 0.20 and pH = 0.70.
scenes.secondary: Trap 2: writing pH = pOH for a base instead of pH = 14 âˆ’ pOH, which wrongly gives an acidic answer. Trap 3: using only the acid's volume when mixing. The excess spreads through V(acid) + V(base).
scenes.callout: Double diprotics; finish bases with 14 âˆ’ pOH; use the total volume.
scenes.caption: Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„ pH 0.70 not 1.0; pH = 14 âˆ’ pOH; use V(acid)+V(base).

voiceover.text: Three traps. Trap one, giving nought-point-one molar sulfuric acid a pH of one. Sulfuric acid is diprotic, so the hydronium concentration is twice the concentration, nought-point-two, and the pH is minus the log of that, nought-point-seven-zero, not one. Always double for dilute sulfuric acid, and the same for calcium and barium hydroxide. Trap two, finishing a base calculation by writing pH equals pOH. That gives an acidic-looking answer for a base, which the sanity check should catch instantly. The correct final step is pH equals fourteen minus pOH. Trap three, in a mixing problem, dividing the excess moles by only the acid's volume, or only the base's. The leftover ion is spread through the combined volume, so always use the volume of acid plus the volume of base.

scenes.heading: Quick check
scenes.question: 25.0 mL of 0.200 mol Lâپ»آ¹ HCl is diluted to 500 mL. Find the new pH.
answerSteps: Moles are conserved on dilution: n(HCl) = 0.200 أ— 0.0250 = 5.00 أ— 10âپ»آ³ mol
Use câ‚پVâ‚پ = câ‚‚Vâ‚‚: new c = 5.00 أ— 10âپ»آ³ أ· 0.500 = 0.0100 mol Lâپ»آ¹
HCl is strong and monoprotic, so [Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛] = 0.0100 mol Lâپ»آ¹
Answer: pH = 2.00 (a 20أ— dilution raised it from 0.70 by ~1.3) âœ“
scenes.caption: Dilution: câ‚‚ = 0.0100 mol Lâپ»آ¹, so pH = 2.00.

voiceover.text: Quick check. Twenty-five millilitres of nought-point-two molar hydrochloric acid is diluted to five hundred millilitres. Find the new pH. Pause and work it through. Right, let's check it. On dilution the moles of acid don't change, you're only adding water, so first find them, nought-point-two times nought-point-zero-two-five, which is five times ten to the minus three moles. Then use c-one V-one equals c-two V-two, or just divide those moles by the new volume, five times ten to the minus three over nought-point-five, giving nought-point-zero-one molar. Hydrochloric acid is strong and monoprotic, so the hydronium concentration is that same nought-point-zero-one. The pH is minus the log, which is two-point-zero-zero. And notice the acid is still strong, it's just more dilute. Dilution moves the pH up toward seven, but never past it.

scenes.heading: Lock it in
points: pH = âˆ’log[Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛]; each unit is a tenfold change in Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛.
Strong acid: [Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛] = c, but double it for dilute Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„.
Strong base: [OHâپ»] = c (أ—2 for group 2), then pH = 14 âˆ’ pOH.
Dilution conserves moles: use câ‚پVâ‚پ = câ‚‚Vâ‚‚, then recalculate pH.
Mixing: find the excess, divide by V(acid) + V(base).
scenes.finalPrompt: Before logging: how many protons or hydroxides per formula unit?
scenes.caption: Five takeaways: log scale, [Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛]=c (أ—2 diprotic), pOH route, dilution, mixing.

voiceover.text: Let's lock it in. Five things to take away. First, pH is minus the log of the hydronium concentration, and each pH unit is a tenfold change in hydrogen ions. Second, for a strong acid the hydronium concentration equals the concentration, but double it for dilute sulfuric acid. Third, for a strong base, find the hydroxide concentration, doubling for calcium or barium hydroxide, then finish with pH equals fourteen minus pOH. Fourth, dilution conserves moles, so use c-one V-one equals c-two V-two before recalculating the pH. And fifth, for mixing, find the excess of acid or base, then divide by the combined volume. Next lesson, we tackle the harder case, the pH of weak acids and bases, using Ka, Kb, and ICE tables. See you there.
